expanded_description: Isosceles and equilateral triangles possess special properties that make them central to geometric proofs. The Base Angles Theorem states that if two sides of a triangle are congruent, the angles opposite those sides are congruent. Its converse states that if two angles are congruent, the sides opposite them are congruent. For equilateral triangles, these theorems yield the powerful result that equilateral and equiangular are equivalent properties‚ÄĒeach implies the other, and all angles measure exactly 60¬į.

core_concept.principle: The Base Angles Theorem: If two sides of a triangle are congruent, then the angles opposite those sides are congruent. Its converse: If two angles of a triangle are congruent, then the sides opposite those angles are congruent. Together they establish a two-way equivalence: congruent sides if and only if congruent opposite angles. For equilateral triangles, applying this theorem twice proves that all three sides are congruent if and only if all three angles are congruent, and each angle must be 60¬į.
core_concept.explanation: An isosceles triangle has mirror symmetry. The two equal legs support equal base angles‚ÄĒif the legs match, the angles opposite them match too. This works both ways: equal angles force equal opposite sides. For an equilateral triangle, all three sides are equal, so all three angles must be equal. Since the three angles sum to 180¬į, each must be exactly 60¬į. This is why equilateral triangles are so predictable‚ÄĒknowing one side gives all three sides, and knowing one angle (60¬į) gives all three angles. These theorems are among the most frequently used in geometry because isosceles triangles appear everywhere, from bridge trusses to architectural designs.

image.alt: Left: Isosceles triangle with two congruent legs marked, base angles marked with matching arcs. Right: Equilateral triangle with all sides marked congruent and all angles marked 60¬į. Equivalence arrow between equilateral and equiangular.
image.caption: Base Angles Theorem: congruent sides ‚Üí congruent opposite angles. Equilateral: all sides equal ‚Üí all angles 60¬į.

sections.introduction: The Base Angles Theorem is one of the most elegant and frequently used results in geometry. It establishes that in an isosceles triangle, the angles opposite the congruent sides are congruent. This symmetry property is the defining characteristic of isosceles triangles and is proved by constructing the angle bisector of the vertex angle, which creates two congruent triangles by SAS.

introduction_image.alt: Isosceles triangle with legs AB and AC marked congruent. Base BC labeled. Base angles at B and C marked with matching arcs. Vertex angle at A. Theorem statement displayed.
introduction_image.caption: Base Angles Theorem: congruent legs ‚Üí congruent base angles. The isosceles triangle is symmetric.

key_concept.definition: The Base Angles Theorem: If two sides of a triangle are congruent, then the angles opposite those sides are congruent. In an isosceles triangle with $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, the angles opposite these sides are $\angle C$ (opposite $\overline{AB}$) and $\angle B$ (opposite $\overline{AC}$), so $\angle B \cong \angle C$. The congruent sides are called the legs. The third side is the base. The angles at the base are the base angles. The angle between the legs is the vertex angle.
key_concept.context: The theorem is proved by drawing the angle bisector from the vertex angle to the base. This creates two triangles that share the bisector as a common side, have congruent legs by given, and have congruent angles by the bisector. SAS establishes triangle congruence, and CPCTC gives the base angles congruent. This theorem is fundamental to triangle geometry and is used constantly in proofs.

image.alt: Isosceles triangle with angle bisector from A to base BC at D. Two resulting triangles shaded. AB=AC given, ‚ą†BAD=‚ą†CAD (bisector), AD=AD reflexive. SAS ‚Üí triangles congruent ‚Üí ‚ą†B‚ČÖ‚ą†C by CPCTC.
image.caption: Base Angles Theorem proof: angle bisector of vertex angle creates SAS congruence.

process_flow.process: Identify the Congruent Sides and Corresponding Parts
process_flow.description: Determine which two sides are congruent (the legs) and which side is the base. The base angles are the angles opposite the legs.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for tick marks or given congruence statements. The two congruent sides are the legs. The third side is the base. The angle opposite each leg is a base angle‚ÄĒthese are the angles that will be proven congruent. The angle between the legs is the vertex angle.
process_flow.result: Correct identification of legs, base, base angles, and vertex angle.
applications: If $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, then $\angle B$ (opposite $\overline{AC}$) and $\angle C$ (opposite $\overline{AB}$) are the base angles.
The base is the side that is NOT one of the congruent legs.

image.alt: Isosceles triangle with legs marked, base labeled, base angles circled, vertex angle marked.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the legs, base, base angles, and vertex angle.

process_flow.description: Conclude that the base angles are congruent: $\angle B \cong \angle C$. Set their measures equal if solving algebraic problems.
process_flow.mechanism: By the Base Angles Theorem: If $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, then $\angle B \cong \angle C$. In measure form, $m\angle B = m\angle C$. If the vertex angle is known, each base angle equals $(180¬į - \text{vertex}) \div 2$. If a base angle is known, the vertex equals $180¬į - 2(\text{base angle})$.
process_flow.result: Congruent base angles with equal measures.

image.alt: Isosceles triangle with base angles marked congruent. Example calculations shown for vertex 40¬į giving base angles 70¬į, and for base angle 55¬į giving vertex 70¬į.
image.caption: Step 2: Apply the theorem‚ÄĒbase angles are congruent. Set their measures equal.

classifications.type: Converse of the Base Angles Theorem
classifications.value: If two angles of a triangle are congruent, then the sides opposite those angles are congruent.
characteristics: Given $\angle B \cong \angle C$, conclude $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$ (the sides opposite these angles).
Proof uses the angle bisector from $A$ and AAS congruence.
This converse is equally important‚ÄĒit allows proving sides are congruent from angle information.
Together, the theorem and its converse establish: congruent sides $\leftrightarrow$ congruent opposite angles.
classifications.behavior: The converse is used when angle information is given and side congruence needs to be proved. It is the logical reverse of the Base Angles Theorem and is equally valid.

examples.process: Using the converse to prove a triangle is isosceles
examples.explanation:
Given $\angle P \cong \angle Q$ in $\triangle PQR$.

By the Converse of the Base Angles Theorem, the sides opposite these angles are congruent: $\overline{QR} \cong \overline{PR}$.

Therefore $\triangle PQR$ is isosceles with legs $\overline{QR}$ and $\overline{PR}$, and base $\overline{PQ}$.

image.alt: Triangle with two congruent angles marked. Sides opposite those angles marked with matching tick marks. Converse statement displayed.
image.caption: Converse: congruent angles ‚Üí congruent opposite sides ‚Üí triangle is isosceles.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the Base Angles Theorem, its converse, the SAS proof, and algebraic applications.
image.caption: Base Angles Theorem overview: the two-way relationship between congruent sides and congruent angles.

sections.introduction: Equilateral triangles‚ÄĒtriangles with all three sides congruent‚ÄĒare the most symmetric of all triangles. By applying the Base Angles Theorem twice, we can prove that all three angles are congruent, and since they sum to 180¬į, each must be exactly 60¬į. The converse also holds: an equiangular triangle must be equilateral.

introduction_image.alt: Equilateral triangle with all sides marked congruent. All three angles marked 60¬į. Logical flow: all sides ‚ČÖ ‚Üí Base Angles Theorem twice ‚Üí all angles ‚ČÖ ‚Üí each 60¬į. Converse flow also shown.
introduction_image.caption: Equilateral triangle: all sides equal ‚Üí all angles 60¬į. Equiangular ‚Üí all angles 60¬į ‚Üí all sides equal.

key_concept.definition: Equilateral Triangle Theorem: A triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular. In an equilateral triangle, all three sides are congruent, all three angles are congruent, and each angle measures exactly $60¬į$. Proof: Given $\overline{AB} = \overline{BC}$, the Base Angles Theorem gives $\angle A = \angle C$. Given $\overline{BC} = \overline{CA}$, the Base Angles Theorem gives $\angle B = \angle A$. Therefore $\angle A = \angle B = \angle C$. Since their sum is $180¬į$, each equals $60¬į$.
key_concept.context: This makes equilateral triangles completely predictable. Given one side, all three sides are known. Given one angle (60¬į), all three are known. This predictability makes equilateral triangles ideal for tessellations, truss designs, and geometric constructions.

image.alt: Equilateral triangle with all sides and angles marked. Proof chain: AB=BC ‚Üí ‚ą†A=‚ą†C. BC=CA ‚Üí ‚ą†B=‚ą†A. Therefore ‚ą†A=‚ą†B=‚ą†C=60¬į.
image.caption: Equilateral ‚ÜĒ Equiangular. Each angle = 60¬į. Proved by the Base Angles Theorem applied twice.

worked_examples.title: Isosceles and Equilateral Triangle Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) In isosceles $\triangle ABC$ with $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, $m\angle A = 50¬į$. Find $m\angle B$ and $m\angle C$.
2) In $\triangle PQR$, $m\angle P = m\angle Q = 70¬į$. Classify the triangle by sides and find $m\angle R$.
3) An equilateral triangle has perimeter $36$ cm. Find each side length and each angle measure.
4) In isosceles $\triangle XYZ$ with $\overline{XY} \cong \overline{XZ}$, $m\angle X = 4x - 10$ and $m\angle Y = 2x + 30$. Find all angle measures.

step_by_step.step: 1) Find base angles from vertex angle
step_by_step.explanation:
Vertex angle $m\angle A = 50¬į$. Sum of angles $= 180¬į$.
$50¬į + m\angle B + m\angle C = 180¬į$. By Base Angles Theorem: $m\angle B = m\angle C$.
$50¬į + 2(m\angle B) = 180¬į$ ‚Üí $2(m\angle B) = 130¬į$ ‚Üí $m\angle B = 65¬į$, $m\angle C = 65¬į$.

$m\angle P = m\angle Q = 70¬į$. By Converse of Base Angles Theorem, sides opposite these angles are congruent ‚Üí $\overline{QR} \cong \overline{PR}$ ‚Üí isosceles.
$m\angle R = 180¬į - 70¬į - 70¬į = 40¬į$.
Classification: Isosceles triangle (legs $\overline{QR}$ and $\overline{PR}$, base $\overline{PQ}$, vertex angle $40¬į$).

Perimeter $= 36$ cm. Equilateral ‚Üí all sides equal.
Each side $= 36 \div 3 = 12$ cm.
Equilateral ‚Üí equiangular ‚Üí each angle $= 60¬į$.

$\overline{XY} \cong \overline{XZ}$ ‚Üí base angles are $\angle Y$ and $\angle Z$. Vertex angle is $\angle X$.
By Base Angles Theorem: $m\angle Y = m\angle Z = 2x + 30$.
Angle sum: $(4x - 10) + (2x + 30) + (2x + 30) = 180¬į$
$8x + 50 = 180¬į$ ‚Üí $8x = 130¬į$ ‚Üí $x = 16.25$
$m\angle X = 4(16.25) - 10 = 55¬į$
$m\angle Y = 2(16.25) + 30 = 62.5¬į$
$m\angle Z = 62.5¬į$
Check: $55¬į + 62.5¬į + 62.5¬į = 180¬į$ ‚úď.

image.alt: Four triangles with solutions: (1) Isosceles with vertex 50¬į, base 65¬į. (2) Isosceles with base angles 70¬į, vertex 40¬į. (3) Equilateral with side 12 cm, angles 60¬į. (4) Isosceles with algebraic expressions, angles 55¬į, 62.5¬į, 62.5¬į.
image.caption: Isosceles and equilateral triangle problems: finding angles, classifying, and solving with algebra.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) $m\angle B = m\angle C = 65¬į$.
2) Isosceles, $m\angle R = 40¬į$.
3) Each side = 12 cm, each angle = 60¬į.
4) $m\angle X = 55¬į$, $m\angle Y = m\angle Z = 62.5¬į$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Base Angles Theorem, its converse, equilateral properties, and algebraic angle equations.

sections.introduction: Mastery of isosceles and equilateral triangle theorems requires practice applying the Base Angles Theorem and its converse, using the 60¬į guarantee, and solving algebraic problems. Work through these problems to build comprehensive fluency.

worked_examples.title: Isosceles and Equilateral Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) An isosceles triangle has a vertex angle of $80¬į$. Find the base angles.
2) An isosceles triangle has a base angle of $35¬į$. Find the vertex angle and classify by angles.
3) In $\triangle DEF$, $\overline{DE} \cong \overline{DF}$ and $m\angle D = 3x$, $m\angle E = 2x + 10$. Find all angle measures.
4) An equilateral triangle has side length $2a + 1$. Its perimeter is $27$. Find $a$ and the side length.
5) True or False: All equilateral triangles are isosceles. Explain.

step_by_step.explanation:
Base angles are congruent. Sum $= 180¬į$.
$80¬į + 2(\text{base}) = 180¬į$ ‚Üí $2(\text{base}) = 100¬į$ ‚Üí base $= 50¬į$ each.

Both base angles $= 35¬į$. Vertex $= 180¬į - 35¬į - 35¬į = 110¬į$.
One angle $> 90¬į$ ‚Üí obtuse triangle. Classification: Isosceles obtuse triangle.

$\overline{DE} \cong \overline{DF}$ ‚Üí base angles are $\angle E$ and $\angle F$ ‚Üí $m\angle E = m\angle F = 2x + 10$.
Angle sum: $3x + (2x+10) + (2x+10) = 180¬į$
$7x + 20 = 180¬į$ ‚Üí $7x = 160¬į$ ‚Üí $x = \frac{160}{7}$
$m\angle D = 3(\frac{160}{7}) = \frac{480}{7} \approx 68.6¬į$
$m\angle E = m\angle F = 2(\frac{160}{7}) + 10 = \frac{320}{7} + 10 = \frac{390}{7} \approx 55.7¬į$
Check: $\frac{480}{7} + \frac{390}{7} + \frac{390}{7} = \frac{1260}{7} = 180¬į$ ‚úď.

TRUE. An equilateral triangle has three congruent sides, which means it has at least two congruent sides‚ÄĒthe definition of isosceles. Equilateral triangles are a special case of isosceles triangles. (Some textbooks define isosceles as exactly two equal sides, in which case the answer would be false. In this course, isosceles means at least two.)

image.alt: Five panels: (1) Vertex 80¬į, base 50¬į each. (2) Base 35¬į, vertex 110¬į obtuse. (3) Algebraic isosceles with angles solved. (4) Equilateral with a=4, side=9. (5) Equilateral inside isosceles Venn diagram.
image.caption: Isosceles and equilateral practice: angle finding, algebra, and classification.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Base angles = 50¬į each.
2) Vertex = 110¬į; isosceles obtuse triangle.
3) $x = 160/7$, $m\angle D \approx 68.6¬į$, base angles $\approx 55.7¬į$ each.
4) $a = 4$, side length = 9.
5) True (with 'at least two' definition of isosceles).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Base Angles Theorem, equilateral 60¬į property, angle sum, and algebraic problem-solving.

summary: Isosceles and equilateral triangles have unique and powerful properties. Key points: 1) Base Angles Theorem: If two sides of a triangle are congruent, the angles opposite them are congruent. The congruent sides are legs; the third side is the base; the angles at the base are the base angles. 2) Converse: If two angles of a triangle are congruent, the sides opposite them are congruent. Together: congruent sides ‚ÜĒ congruent opposite angles. 3) Equilateral Triangle Theorem: A triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular. All three sides congruent ‚ÜĒ all three angles congruent ‚ÜĒ each angle = 60¬į. Proved by applying the Base Angles Theorem twice. 4) Algebraic Applications: Use the equality of base angles to set up equations. For equilateral triangles, all angles are 60¬į and all sides are equal. 5) Classification: Isosceles triangles can be acute, right, or obtuse. Equilateral triangles are always acute (all 60¬į). 6) These theorems are among the most frequently used in geometry proofs, especially in triangle congruence and properties of polygons.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles' in center. Branches: Base Angles Theorem (congruent sides ‚Üí congruent base angles, proof via SAS), Converse (congruent angles ‚Üí congruent opposite sides), Equilateral Theorem (equilateral ‚ÜĒ equiangular, all angles 60¬į), Applications (find angles, solve algebra, classify triangles).
review_questions_image.caption: Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles Review: the Base Angles Theorem, its converse, and the 60¬į equilateral guarantee.
